steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage explains that a larger size difference between worker and drone cells forces bees to use more nonhexagonal filler cells. The graph shows western honeybee has far fewer nonhexagonal cells (0.1–0.5%) than dwarf honeybee (2.1–2.6%) or black dwarf honeybee (2.4–2.5%). The conclusion must use this data to make a logical inference.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — cells for worker eggs are probably closer in size to cells for drone eggs in the hives of the western honeybee than in the hives of the dwarf honeybee and the black dwarf honeybee.: Fewer nonhexagonal cells means a smaller size difference between worker and drone cells — exactly what the data supports. ✓
  • Choice B — both the western honeybee and the black dwarf honeybee probably reserve eight-sided cells for drone eggs, while the dwarf honeybee likely deposits drone eggs in seven-sided cells.: The graph shows percentages of nonhexagonal cells, not the specific shapes used. This cannot be inferred. ✗
  • Choice C — the western honeybee probably relies on many more geometrical shapes when constructing cells than either the dwarf honeybee or the black dwarf honeybee does.: The opposite is true — western honeybee has fewer nonhexagonal cells, meaning it uses fewer geometrical shapes. ✗
  • Choice D — the percentage of hexagonal cells is probably slightly lower in the hives of the western honeybee than in the hives of the dwarf honeybee and the black dwarf honeybee.: Since western honeybee has fewer nonhexagonal cells, it actually has a higher percentage of hexagonal cells, not lower. ✗
A is correct.
